projects.name: Low-Latency LOB Matching Engine
projects.description: High-frequency limit order book matching engine targeting sub-microsecond latency using SIMD-optimized price level traversal and lock-free queue architecture.
projects.positioning: Sub-microsecond order matching for high-frequency trading infrastructure
projects.architectureOverview: Lock-free MPSC queue for order ingestion feeding into a price-level indexed order book with SIMD-accelerated traversal. Separate execution engine with atomic operations for fill matching.

challenges.challenge: False sharing between bid and ask sides causing cache misses at high throughput
challenges.solution: Aligned bid/ask structures to separate cache lines with explicit padding

challenges.challenge: Lock contention on order ID generation under load
challenges.solution: Switched to atomic fetch-and-add with relaxed memory ordering for ID allocation

caseStudy.problem: Manual order matching in HFT systems is the critical bottleneck. Every microsecond of latency in order processing translates directly to worse fill prices and missed opportunities. Building a matching engine that operates below 1μs requires careful co-design of data structures, memory layout, and CPU micro-architecture awareness.
constraints: No heap allocation in hot path
Single-threaded order book with concurrent ingestion queue
decisions: Lock-free MPSC queue over mutex-guarded queue — eliminates contention on producer side
Price levels as sorted arrays over red-black tree — better cache locality for common case
SIMD for price search over sequential scan — 4x speedup on AVX2
Custom allocator for order nodes — avoids malloc overhead in critical path
tradeoffs: Arrays for price levels: O(n) insertion vs O(log n) for tree, but cache locality wins for typical book depth < 100 levels
Lock-free queue: harder to reason about, but eliminates 200-400ns mutex overhead per order
Single-threaded book: simplifies correctness, sacrifices parallelism
metrics: 4x price search speedup vs sequential via SIMD
caseStudy.retrospective: I'd add a formal latency histogram output to the benchmark harness earlier — I wasted time debugging performance regressions that a proper histogram would have caught immediately. I'd also explore prefetching next price level during SIMD search.

projects.name: High-Frequency Market Making Simulator
projects.description: Tick-by-tick market making simulation with dynamic bid-ask spread quoting, inventory-based quote skewing, and stochastic execution model across 5,000 price ticks.
projects.architectureOverview: Event-driven simulation engine with a stochastic price process, inventory-aware quoting strategy, and execution model with fill probability based on order flow toxicity.

challenges.challenge: Inventory blowup under trending markets — one-sided fill pressure
challenges.solution: Added inventory risk penalty to spread formula: spread += γ * |inventory| * σ

challenges.challenge: Overfit strategy to specific volatility regime
challenges.solution: Tested across multiple σ regimes; added realized volatility adaptation

caseStudy.problem: Market makers profit from the bid-ask spread but face inventory risk when markets trend. Building a simulator that accurately models execution uncertainty, adverse selection, and inventory dynamics is essential for strategy development without risking real capital.
constraints: Must model realistic fill probabilities (not deterministic)
P&L attribution must separate spread capture from inventory moves
decisions: Stochastic execution model over deterministic — more realistic; fills depend on queue position
Inventory-aware skewing over symmetric quoting — reduces adverse selection
Realized volatility for regime detection over implied — simpler, no options data needed
tradeoffs: Stochastic fills: harder to backtest reproducibly, but necessary for realistic strategy evaluation
Inventory skewing: reduces adverse selection but widens effective spread, hurting fill rate
metrics: Positive Sharpe under low and medium volatility regimes
Max drawdown contained to <3% of notional under tested regimes
caseStudy.retrospective: The execution model is still too simple — real HFT execution depends on queue position, which requires a proper limit order book simulation. The next version should integrate with the LOB matching engine.

projects.name: CLI Task Manager
projects.description: Production-grade CLI task manager with 9 Typer commands, JSON-backed persistence, dataclass serialization, automatic backup, and single-command undo — zero data loss across destructive operations.
projects.architectureOverview: 4-module separation: CLI layer (Typer commands) → business logic → models (dataclasses) → storage (JSON with atomic write + backup). Single-command undo via backup file restoration.

challenges.challenge: Destructive operations (delete, bulk-delete) risking data loss if interrupted
challenges.solution: Atomic write pattern: write to temp file, rename to target — single-command undo via backup

challenges.challenge: Validating due dates and priorities at the CLI layer without leaking logic
challenges.solution: Validation callbacks in Typer decorators — errors surface before any business logic runs
